<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: executable-specifications-and-lean-proof-envelope
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/executable-specifications-and-lean-proof-envelope.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/executable-specifications-and-lean-proof-envelope.qmd@8f1a27c5a
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# Executable Specifications and Lean Proof Envelope

Formal language is powerful because it can make a claim smaller. A theorem, a
schema, a validator, and a benchmark each narrow a different kind of promise.
The proof envelope exists so those promises do not blur together.

The reader should not have to guess what kind of authority a formal-looking
sentence carries. If a local Lean predicate builds, that says something precise
about a finite record. If a JSON Schema accepts a fixture, that says something
about record shape. If a validator passes, that says something about the exact
condition the validator checks. None of those facts automatically proves source
truth, deployed enforcement, benchmark performance, model quality, or safety.

This chapter is about that boundary. It gives the stack a way to say "this was
checked" without letting checked things become larger than they are. The mature
version is not a grand proof of the whole ASI Stack. It is a claims-control
plane that keeps every formal artifact attached to its lane, command, consumer,
limitation, and non-claim.

## Problem

The book contains many claims that invite formal treatment: authority ceilings,
support-state transitions, stable capability replacement, job lifecycles,
context adequacy, proof-carrying claims, and cyclic contracts. Some of those
claims can become Lean predicates. Some belong in JSON Schemas. Some are better
handled by process validators, behavior tests, or benchmark records. Some are
still research questions.

The failure mode is not lack of rigor. The failure mode is misplaced rigor.
"The architecture is safe" is too broad to prove inside this repository.
"A support-state transition cannot move above `argument` without the required
evidence record" is narrow enough to encode. "This schema validates a receipt"
is useful, but it does not prove the event behind the receipt happened. "This
theorem reference exists in an external project" may orient the design, but it
is not local proof ownership.

The proof envelope is the discipline that keeps those differences visible. It
turns formal ambition into routing: Lean when the predicate is operational,
schema validation when the record shape matters, process validation when
cross-file wiring matters, behavior tests when a path must be exercised,
benchmarks when measured performance is at issue, and backlog when the claim is
not ready.

## Why Existing Approaches Are Insufficient

Formal methods are often discussed as if the only question is whether a system
uses them. That is not enough. The harder question is whether the proof matches
the boundary it is being asked to defend. A correct finite predicate can still
be the wrong predicate for the prose claim.

External formal-methods families already show why one generic proof bucket is
too crude. PDDL models planning domains and problems. Proof-carrying code pairs
executable artifacts with checkable evidence. TLA+ models systems. Lean supplies
proof-assistant structure. Dafny connects programs and specifications. Reluplex
verifies neural-network properties. Black-Box Simplex, Copilot, and PRISM sit
in runtime assurance, monitoring, and model-checking families. The ASI Stack
uses those as external baselines for lane separation, not as proof that the
local architecture works.

The same separation matters inside this repository. A Lean module may prove a
record invariant, while a schema validates fields, while a script checks
traceability, while a benchmark measures a workload. Those artifacts can support
one another, but they cannot silently substitute for one another. When they do,
proof becomes decoration: a rigorous-looking wrapper around a claim whose
semantics were never formalized.

Semantic proof drift is the central risk. A target can compile cleanly while
the chapter, source interpretation, or consumer expectation has moved on. The
answer is not to distrust the proof. The answer is to record semantic adequacy:
whether the finite predicate is the right predicate for the claim that wants to
consume it.

## Core Claim

Lean proofs and executable specs should target small invariants: authority,
support states, SCF replacement, job lifecycle, context adequacy, and residual
records (evidence boundary: architectural argument).

The claim remains at `argument` support. This repository has a Lean workspace,
and its current manifest records 166 proof targets implemented as narrow
finite-record Lean predicates. Current proof-depth snapshot: 166 proof targets, 54 Lean modules, 858 theorem declarations, 682 derived/decomposed, 172 direct/projection, 4 unknown/mixed, and 5/5 safety-critical chapter classifications present. That coverage does not prove broad chapter claims,
source interpretation, deployed enforcement, model quality, benchmark
performance, semantic adequacy, or ASI safety.

The counts are useful because they make proof etiquette visible. A
`derived/decomposed` theorem has at least some structural proof work inside the
finite model. A `direct/projection` theorem mostly exposes a field or implication
already present in the record. Both can be useful, but they do not carry the
same persuasive weight. The phrase projection-only traceability is there to
keep the modest cases honest. The proof-depth report is not a proof of semantic
adequacy; it is a warning label and a maintenance check.

## Mechanism

The proof envelope begins by refusing to formalize the book's broad theses
directly. A broad claim is first lowered into a Proof Target Record. That record
names the chapter, tag, target kind, artifact lane, command, verifier result,
consumer requirements, semantic adequacy state, limitations, and non-claims.

Only after that routing step does implementation matter. If the target is a
Lean-suitable operational predicate, it belongs in a Lean module and must pass
the local build. If it is a record-shape claim, it belongs in a schema and
fixture. If it is a wiring or release-discipline claim, it may belong in a
process validator. If it is about behavior or measured performance, it needs a
test or benchmark lane. If it is too broad or underspecified, it stays planned,
blocked, or residual.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
A["Chapter claim or invariant"] --> B["Proof Target Record"]
B --> C["Triage kind and non-claims"]
C --> D{"Operational predicate?"}
D -- "Lean-suitable" --> E["Lean module and lake build"]
D -- "record or process" --> F["Schema, fixture, or validator"]
D -- "not operational yet" --> G["Research target, blocked target, or residual"]
E --> H{"Verifier passed?"}
F --> H
H -- "yes" --> I["Narrow implemented proof/spec state"]
H -- "no" --> G
I --> J["Appendix E and proof manifest"]
J --> K["Consumer checks adequacy and non-claims"]
K -- "adequate for use" --> L["Scoped consumption"]
K -- "not adequate" --> G
```

**What the proof envelope shows:** a proof or executable spec is not consumed
directly from prose. It passes through a target record, artifact lane, verifier,
and semantic-adequacy boundary before any later chapter or release surface can
borrow its authority.

The current implementation is intentionally narrow. `AsiStackProofs.ProofEnvelope`
models finite proof-target status discipline: an implemented target must carry
module-existence and build-passed flags, and a non-operational target is routed
to planned or blocked rather than implemented. That is useful record logic. It
is not a filesystem crawler, theorem-discovery tool, external theorem
validator, deployed enforcement check, or proof that the whole stack behaves
correctly.

The companion note for this chapter calls the lanes by ordinary names: Lean
predicate, schema validation, process validator, behavior test, benchmark,
external theorem reference, semantic adequacy review, and research backlog.
The curated reader spine keeps the same distinction because it is
meaning-critical. Moving the vocabulary entirely into a companion note would
make the chapter easier to read but less honest.

## Interfaces

The Proof Target Record is the interface between prose, outline, Lean, schemas,
validators, Appendix E, and future release profiles. It lets the book say which
finite predicate has an artifact behind it while keeping broader system
behavior outside the proof claim.

Minimum fields:

- `proof_id`
- `chapter_id`
- `lean_tag`
- `target_kind`
- `artifact_lane`
- `formal_target`
- `artifact_refs`
- `verifier_command`
- `verifier_result`
- `support_state_effect`
- `consumer_requirements`
- `semantic_adequacy_review`
- `limitations`
- `non_claims`

The support-state effect is deliberately narrow. A passed `lake build` can
justify saying that a local finite-record predicate builds. It cannot justify
saying that source interpretation is correct, that a real deployment enforces
the rule, that a model behaves well, or that a benchmark improved. A passed
schema validator can justify saying that a fixture has the required shape. It
cannot justify saying the represented event happened.

Consumer requirements belong beside the artifact. A chapter, protocol, runtime
module, or release profile may consume a proof only if the artifact lane,
command, version, adequacy state, and non-claims match the consumer's use. A
proof can be true and still unusable for a particular downstream claim.

## Invariants

The proof envelope keeps formal artifacts in their proper lane:

- no theorem is claimed proven without a passing `lake build`;
- proof tags remain stable and traceable from the outline;
- broad claims are decomposed before formalization;
- schemas, Python fixtures, diagrams, and Quarto renders are not Lean proofs;
- proof status does not imply model quality or deployment safety;
- external theorem IDs are not local proof ownership unless imported and
  validated under the repository's rules;
- semantic adequacy is required before a finite predicate can strengthen prose
  beyond its exact boundary.

These invariants make proof useful by making it modest. A proof that cannot
name its limitation is not ready to carry authority through the stack.

## Failure Modes

The first failure mode is formalization theater: a broad thesis is made to look
settled because it is near a theorem, schema, diagram, or validator. The second
is proof laundering: a valid local predicate, external theorem ID, generated
receipt, or schema pass is used to promote a broader claim whose semantics were
never formalized.

The third failure is orphaned formality. A proof target may still compile while
the prose no longer explains it, the outline no longer routes to it, or the
source claim it was meant to bound has changed. In that case the theorem may be
valid but no longer govern the manuscript.

The fourth failure is semantic mismatch. The finite predicate is true, but it
does not match the boundary a later chapter or release artifact wants to rely
on. That gap should become an adequacy residual, downgrade, quarantine, or
research target. It should not be hidden behind a green build.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The minimum viable proof envelope already has several public pieces. The
generated proof manifest records proof tags from the outline. The
`proof_target_record` fixture validates chapter ID, Lean tag, target kind,
artifact lane, formal target, artifact references, verifier command/result,
support-state effect, consumer requirements, semantic adequacy state,
limitations, and non-claims. The local verification gate includes
`cd lean && lake build`. Appendix E publishes the generated coverage summary
from `proofs/proof_triage.json`. The proof artifact traceability audit checks
that proof targets are wired through manifest records, triage records, Lean
modules, root imports, chapter hooks, limitation prose, and Appendix E
coverage.

That is a useful minimum. It is still not semantic proof adequacy. The live book
therefore keeps the chapter core claim at `argument` support. The next honest
slice is a proof/spec receipt suite: one Lean predicate receipt, one
schema-validation receipt, one process-validator receipt, one behavior-test
receipt, one benchmark receipt, one external theorem reference rejected as
local proof, and one semantic adequacy residual that blocks prose promotion.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The mature endpoint is a claims control plane for formal artifacts. Every
formal-looking promise in the stack would have to travel with the artifact that
supports it, the command that checked it, the boundary it covers, the consumer
allowed to use it, and the claims it refuses to support.

In that endpoint, chapters, protocols, releases, and runtime modules would
consume proof only through typed records. Lean predicates, schemas, process
checks, behavior tests, benchmarks, external theorem references, and research
backlog items would remain different objects with different authority. A
consumer gate would block downstream use when lane, command, version, adequacy
state, or non-claims do not match the proposed use.

The most important future work is semantic adequacy. A predicate can be valid
and still be wrong for the prose boundary. A mature proof envelope would catch
stale theorem names, orphaned targets, borrowed theorem IDs, mismatched
consumer needs, and claim-from-prose errors as downgrade or residual events
instead of letting them become confidence theater.

That endpoint remains a target architecture. It has not been demonstrated as a
deployed proof-control system. The current repository has narrow finite-record
proofs, schema fixtures, traceability checks, and public non-claims. Stronger
claims need semantic adequacy reviews, artifact audits, runtime consumers, and
accepted evidence transitions.

## Summary

The proof envelope makes formality useful by keeping it small, operational, and
bounded. Lean proves only what Lean checks. Schemas validate shape. Tests
exercise behavior. Benchmarks measure recorded workloads. External theorem
references orient the work only when their import and replay status are
explicit.

The chapter's discipline is simple: no artifact may speak outside its lane
without a reviewed transition. That keeps formal methods from becoming
ornamental confidence and gives future agents a precise standard for improving
the book.

If the proof envelope works, a reader can tell what was proved, what was merely
validated, what was tested, what was benchmarked, what was borrowed as external
context, and what remains prose. That separation is what makes formal machinery
trustworthy enough to sit inside a living book.

## Handoff

Executable specifications and Lean predicates define which narrow claims can be
checked, but evidence pressure also needs a path for measured capability without
Goodhart collapse. Benchmark Ratchets and Anti-Goodhart Evidence takes that
role. It turns benchmark runs into records with baselines, residuals,
regressions, saturation states, contamination boundaries, and promotion
decisions so measured pressure cannot outrun preserved failures.
